metadata.title: Extraction Schema First: Structure Confers Credibility
metadata.description_blurb: Extraction without a schema means Cowork invents the rules. A clean-looking table with wrong values is worse than an obvious failure — structure confers credibility whether the values are right or not.

beats.narration_text: She asked Cowork to extract expense data from forty receipts into a spreadsheet. Cowork produced a clean table — vendor names, dates, totals, categories. The table looked authoritative. Three weeks later, during reconciliation, the accountant found the vendor 'CVS' in some rows and 'CVS Pharmacy' in others, three totals that did not match the receipts they referenced, and a category column that had invented names from no taxonomy she recognized. The table was clean. The values were wrong. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, extract expense data from receipts

props.command: extract expense data from these forty receipts into a spreadsheet

beats.narration_text: Extraction and summarization look different in the output, but only one of them claims to represent what the source documents said. A summary can be approximately right and still be useful. An extracted value claims to be the number on the receipt. A clean-formatted table with wrong values is worse than an obvious failure — it passes through review because it looks right. Structure confers credibility whether the values are correct or not.
beats.new_visual_element: Structure credibility — clean table looks authoritative / wrong values pass review

beats.narration_text: The schema defines what to extract, what to do with ambiguous cases, and how to handle values that cannot be found. Column names and their types. Exact vocabulary for categories — not invented names. What to put when a value is missing or unreadable. Whether to flag ambiguous values or exclude them. Without a schema, Cowork invents one. It may be internally consistent on good days. It will never match your reporting requirements.
beats.new_visual_element: Schema fields — column names / types / category vocabulary / missing value handling / ambiguity flag

beats.narration_text: The correct workflow: give Cowork the schema and ask it to produce an extraction plan before touching the data. 'Here is the schema. Here are three sample receipts. Describe how you will handle each field for these samples, and flag any ambiguities.' You read the plan. You correct it. Then you run extraction on all forty. A wrong plan costs ten minutes. A wrong extraction of forty receipts costs a day of reconciliation.
beats.new_visual_element: Plan-before-extract loop — schema + samples → extraction plan → approve → full run
beats.spark_line: Plan on three. Run on forty.

beats.narration_text: After extraction, one verification step that costs five minutes: reconcile the sum of extracted totals against an independent total you already know — a credit card statement, a prior budget line, a sum you can compute. Discrepancies are not always extraction errors, but they are always worth investigating. A wrong extraction that matches your independent total by chance is a coincidence. A discrepancy is information. The reconciliation makes the wrong extraction findable.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — schema first / plan on samples / reconcile sum vs. independent total

beats.narration_text: Write your extraction schema before the next Cowork run. Paste this.

props.command: Help me write an extraction schema before I run the Cowork extraction. My documents: [describe them]. What I need to extract: [describe fields]. For each field, define: (1) column name, (2) data type, (3) exact vocabulary if it is a category, (4) what to put when the value is missing, (5) how to flag ambiguous or unreadable values. Then produce an extraction plan using three sample documents before I approve the full run.

beats.narration_text: If this stops a clean-looking table from hiding wrong values, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
